title: practical english usage - Part 22
content:
The cake tastes wonderful. (     The cake's tasting wondenful. ►    603.) - I'm just tasting the cake to see if it's OK.

Does the meat smell bad? (     Is the meat smelling bad? ►    581.) - Why are you smelling the meat? Is it bad?

I see what you mean, (     I'm seeing what you mean. - see here - I'm seeing Leslie tomorrow.

government? - think here = have an opinion.) - What are you thinking about?

I feel we shouldn't do it. (     I'm feeling we shouldn't do it. - feel here =  have or - I'm feeling fine. (   I feel fine. ►    463.1) above.

Compare the progressive and non-progressive uses of some of the verbs listed 3 progressive         and   non-progressive           uses More details on usage are given in entries for some of these verbs.

depend         matter    weigh (= have weight) contain         lack      possess consist          involve    own concern          include    owe fit belong                   need be             deserve    measure (= have length, etc) other disagree deny           please    surprise astonish         mean      seem appear         look (= seem)     satisfy agree           impress    promise communicating and causing reactions grammar •  6  perfect structures progressive)                                                                                                      -> They have been living in Portugal for the last year. (present perfect Perfect and progressive forms can be combined.

Having seen the film, I don't want to read the book. (perfect -ing form) I'm sorry to have disturbed you. (perfect infinitive) We will have finished by tomorrow afternoon. (future perfect tense) passive) When I went back to the village the house had been pulled down. (past perfect I told him  that I had never heard of the place. (past perfect) She has lost her memory. (present perfect) Perfect verb forms are made with have + past participle.

6 perfect      structures He's always scratching himself in public.

The  structure is often used in critical remarks.

(unexpected, not regular) Her mother was always arranging little surprise picnics and outings.

- When I was a child, we always had picnics on Saturdays in the summer.

I'm always meeting Mrs Bailiff in the supermarket. (accidental, unplanned planned arrangement) - When Alice comes to see me, I always meet her at the station. (a regular, more often than expected), and which  are unexpected or unplanned. Compare: This structure is used to talk about things which  happen very often (perhaps That cat's forever getting shut in the bathroom.

I'm continually running into Oliver these days.

I'm always losing my keys. Granny's always giving people little presents.

mean 'very often'.

We   can use always, continually and similar words with a progressive form to 5 progressive         with    always,        etc You can respect people's views without agreeing with them.

Knowing her tastes, I bought her a large box of chocolates.

can be used in other kinds of structure.

Even verbs which  are never used in progressive tenses have -ing forms which 5 -ing    forms Can you smell something burning?

I can see Emma  coming down the road.

give a kind of progressive meaning, especially in British English (►  84).

Can is often used with see, hear, feel, taste, smell, understand and remember to 4 can    see,    etc 1 Verbs    Section grammar •  7  subjects, objects and complements The door opened.    The curtain's moving.

- She opened the door.   The wind's moving the curtain.

(►   178) verb. Compare: subject; the intransitive use has a meaning rather like a passive (►  9) or reflexive Some verbs are used transitively and intransitively with different kinds of TH send you the form tomorrow.  I'm going to buy Sarah some flowers.

For details, ►    8.

Some transitive verbs can be followed by two objects (indirect and direct).

England lost.         Let's eat.

- England lost the match. - I can't eat this.

Many verbs can be both transitive and intransitive. Compare: but not I usually sleep well. (          She slept the baby.) but not Do sit down. (          Do sit that chair.) 'intransitive'. Examples are sit, sleep.
